title: Precipitation of nickel and cobalt
abstract: A method of precipitating a nickel or cobalt containing solid from an acidic solution containing dissolved nickel and/or cobalt comprises contacting the solution with a calcium containing neutralising agent to raise the pH of the solution to 5.0 to 9.0 at a temperature of from 40 to 95 Â°C to thereby precipitate a solid cobalt containing material and/or a solid nickel containing material and gypsum, and separating the cobalt containing material and/or the nickel containing material from the gypsum. The solid cobalt containing material and/or a solid nickel containing material may report to a fine particle fraction and the gypsum may report to a coarse particle size fraction and the solid cobalt containing material and/or solid nickel containing material may be separated from the gypsum using a particle size separation technique.
claims:
Claims 1. A method of precipitating a nickel or cobalt containing solid from an acidic solution containing dissolved nickel and/or cobalt comprising: a) contacting the solution with a calcium containing neutralising agent to raise the pH of the solution to 5.0 to 9.0 at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C to thereby precipitate a solid cobalt containing material and gypsum, and separating the cobalt containing material from the gypsum; or b) contacting the solution with a calcium containing neutralising agent to raise the pH of the solution to 5.0 to 9.0 at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C to thereby precipitate a solid nickel containing material and gypsum, and separating the nickel containing material from the gypsum; or c) contacting the solution with a calcium containing neutralising agent to raise the pH of the solution to 5.0 to 9.0 at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C to thereby precipitate a solid nickel and cobalt containing material and gypsum, and separating the nickel and cobalt containing material from the gypsum; or d) performing steps a) and b) in a sequence, at different pH to recover separate cobalt and nickel products wherein the cobalt is precipitated in step (a) at a pH between 5.0 to 7.5 and nickel is precipitated in step (b) at a higher pH between 8.0 and 9.0. 2. A method as claimed in claim 1 wherein the acidic solution also contains dissolved magnesium and the precipitation step is operated without causing substantial precipitation of magnesium. 3. A method as claimed in claim 1 or claim 2 wherein in steps (a), (b) or (c) the pH is from 6.0 to 9.0, or from 7.52 8.5. 4. A method as claimed in any one of claims 1 to 3 wherein a temperature of from to 95Â°C, or from 40Â°C to about 60Â°C, or about 60Â°C is used in steps (a), (b) or (c). 5. A method as claimed in any one of the preceding claims wherein the calcium containing neutralising agent is selected from lime (CaO) or hydrated lime (Ca(OH) 2 ), or limestone (CaC0 3 ) or a mixture of two or more thereof. 6. A method as claimed in any one of the preceding claims wherein the solution containing dissolved nickel and cobalt also contain other dissolved metals selected from one or more of copper and zinc and the process includes the step of: (d) contacting the solution with a calcium containing neutralising agent to raise the pH of the solution to 4.5 to 6.0 at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C to precipitate other metals (Cu and/or Zn) to form a solid Cu and/or Zn containing material and gypsum, and separating the Cu and/or Zn containing material from the gypsum, step (d) being conducted before precipitation of nickel and/or cobalt. 7. A method as claimed in claim 6 wherein the solution also contains dissolved magnesium and step (d) is operated without causing substantial precipitation of magnesium. 8. A method as claimed in claim 6 or claim 7 wherein precipitated copper and zinc material and precipitated gypsum is separated from a liquor, with the liquor still containing dissolved nickel and/or cobalt being sent to step (a), (b) or (c). 9. A method as claimed in any one of the preceding claims wherein the method comprises contacting the solution with a calcium containing neutralising agent to raise the pH of the solution to 5.0 to 9.0, or from 6.0 to 9.0, or from 7.5 to 8.5 at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C, or about 60 Â°C, to thereby precipitate a solid nickel and/or cobalt containing material and gypsum and separating the nickel and/or cobalt containing material from the gypsum, wherein the calcium containing neutralising agent is in the form of lime (CaO) or hydrated lime (Ca(OH) 2 ) and nickel and/or cobalt in solution is precipitated in the form of nickel oxide, nickel hydroxide and/or nickel sulphate and/or cobalt oxide, cobalt hydroxide and/or cobalt sulphate. 10. A method as claimed in claim 9 wherein following precipitation, a pulp is formed and the pulp is sent to a separation process in order to separate the nickel and/or cobalt precipitate from gypsum. 11. A method as claimed in claim 10 wherein the pulp is subjected to a solid/liquid separation step to reduce a liquid content of the pulp. 12. A method as claimed in any one of the preceding claims wherein nickel containing material and/or cobalt containing material is separated from the gypsum using a flotation step. 13. A method as claimed in any one of claims 1 to 12 wherein the precipitation step results in the nickel containing material and/or cobalt containing material forms a finer fraction of particles and the gypsum forms a coarser fraction of particles and the finer particles which contain most of the precipitated nickel and/or cobalt are separated from the coarser particles which contain most of the gypsum using a size separation process. 14. A method as claimed in claim 13 wherein the size separation process is selected from classification, elutriation, settling, screening, tables, and cyclones. 15. A method as claimed in claim 13 or claim 14 wherein control of the size of the gypsum particles is achieved by feeding gypsum seed particles to the precipitation stage. 16. A method as claimed in claim 15 wherein the gypsum seed particles comprise a recycled stream of gypsum fines that are recovered from the precipitation stage. 17. A method as claimed in any one of claims 13 to 16 wherein the coarser fraction is leached with acid to dissolve nickel and/or cobalt and the nickel and/or cobalt bearing solution is recycled to steps (a), (b) or (c) to recover nickel and/or cobalt. 18. A method as claimed in claim 17 wherein a leached residue from leaching of the coarser fraction is washed and recovered as pure gypsum. 19. A method as claimed in any one of claims 1 to 12 wherein the process comprises contacting the solution with a calcium carbonate containing neutralising agent to raise the 

 pH of the solution to 5.0 to 7.5, or from 6.5 to 7.5, at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C preferably about 60Â°C, to precipitate a solid nickel and/or cobalt containing material and gypsum without causing substantial precipitation of magnesium, and separating the solid nickel and/or cobalt containing material from the gypsum, the calcium containing neutralising agent comprising limestone (CaC0 3 ) and the nickel and/or cobalt in solution is precipitated as nickel and/or cobalt carbonate. 20. A method as claimed in claim 19 wherein milled limestone is contacted with nickel and/or cobalt sulphate solution at pH 5.0 to 7.0, or pH 6.0 - 7.0 at a temperature of from 40 to 95Â°C, preferably about 60Â°C, the milled limestone having a nominal size of less than ÎÎÎÎ¼Î¹Î·, to cause nickel and/or cobalt carbonate and gypsum to precipitate, and a slurry formed during precipitation being passed to a separator a finer fraction of particles from a coarser fraction of particles, and the coarser fraction of particles that includes a significant proportion of the precipitated gypsum and unreacted limestone is sent to nickel and/or cobalt polishing or for solid/liquid separation and the finer fraction of particles that includes a significant proportion of the precipitated nickel and/or cobalt carbonate is partly recycled to another reactor or directly to a reaction vessel to contact all or a portion of the incoming fresh nickel and/or cobalt containing solution whereby any unreacted limestone from nickel and/or cobalt precipitation is converted to gypsum and a slurry from this stage is sent to a second separator and the liquid stream which still contains dissolved nickel and/or cobalt is sent for nickel and/or cobalt precipitation with limestone. 21. A method as claimed in claim 20 wherein an underflow from the second separator is sent to nickel and/or cobalt solution polishing to remove any nickel and/or cobalt from solution, or a slurry is sent to size separation where any precipitated nickel and/or cobalt carbonate reports to a fine fraction and a coarse gypsum fraction is recycled to a precipitation step. 22. A method as claimed in claim 19 wherein the precipitation step results in a slurry containing precipitated nickel and/or cobalt carbonate and gypsum being formed, the slurry being separated into a nickel and/or cobalt carbonate -rich fraction and a gypsum- 

 rich fraction, the gypsum-rich fraction containing precipitated gypsum and unreacted limestone, the nickel and/or cobalt carbonate- rich fraction comprising fine particles that include a significant proportion of precipitated nickel and/or cobalt carbonate, and the gypsum-rich fraction comprising coarse particles that include a significant proportion of precipitated gypsum and unreacted limestone, the nickel and/or cobalt carbonate -rich fraction being sent forward to nickel and/or cobalt polishing or for solid/liquid separation, the gypsum-rich fraction being recycled to another reactor or sent directly to a reaction vessel or thickener to contact all or a portion of fresh nickel and/or cobalt sulphate solution from a leach process to convert unreacted limestone to gypsum, a resulting slurry being separated into a liquid stream and a solids stream, the liquid- stream, which contains dissolved nickel and/or cobalt being sent to nickel and/or cobalt precipitation with limestone and the solids stream being sent to nickel and/or cobalt solution polishing to remove any nickel and/or cobalt from solution or sent straight to separation where any precipitated nickel and/or cobalt carbonate is separated from precipitated gypsum, the precipitated gypsum being recycled, the precipitated nickel and/or cobalt carbonate being subjected to a liquid/solid separation and the resulting solid being washed to remove any magnesium in solution, the washed solids being recovered as a nickel and/or cobalt carbonate concentrate. 23. A method as claimed in any one of the preceding claims wherein a the fraction containing cobalt and/or nickel containing material is contacted with a fresh acidic feed solution and held at a pH that is lower than the pH at which the calcium and/or nickel containing material is first precipitated, followed by separating the cobalt and/or nickel containing material from a gypsum containing material. 24. A method as claimed in any one of the preceding claims wherein gypsum that is separated from the nickel and/or cobalt containing material is recycled to step (a), (b) or (c) or (d). 25. A process as claimed in claim 1 wherein cobalt-containing material formed in step (d) is separated from a liquid phase and the liquid phase is then treated to precipitate a nickel containing material therefrom.
